svg.title: The Guide to Wireframing — first 20 pages (FrameForge replica)
svg.desc: Structural replica of pp. 1–20 of UXPin's “The Guide to Wireframing” (Chris Bank). Body text is the real extracted copy; the book's photographic/sketch figures are the authors' copyrighted illustrations, shown as labelled image-placeholders. The cover's wireframe mock is rebuilt from primitives. Layout is representative, not pixel-exact.

text.tspan: Whether you’re aware of it or not, every shade, line, text, graphic,
interaction that you add to a wireframe impacts how people interpret and
understand the product concepts. Like English (or French if you’re looking for
a more romantic language), wireframing is a language - for product design and
The bottom line is that every team develops its own internal language, a slight
variation from the norm, and wireframing is flexible enough to accommodate many
styles to get the job done.
Many pundits may disagree whether or not some of these tools and medium are
relevant to wireframing, but if it looks like a wireframe and smells like a
wireframe, I’m calling it a wireframe. Just get the wireframes done!
